_meta.description: Critical energy infrastructure status for the 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis

facilities.name: Kharg Island Oil Terminal
facilities.notes: 7 oil export jetties destroyed by US B-2/Tomahawk strikes (Mar 3-4). 1.8M bpd export capacity completely offline. Single most significant infrastructure strike of the conflict.

facilities.name: Lavan Island Oil Terminal
facilities.notes: No direct damage reports, but export operations likely halted due to Hormuz closure and insurance unavailability. Shadow fleet may be loading.

facilities.name: Sirri Island Oil Terminal
facilities.notes: No direct damage reports. Export operations likely halted or minimal (shadow fleet only).

facilities.name: Bandar Abbas Refinery
facilities.notes: Struck during Operation Epic Fury (Mar 3-4). Feedstock supply severed after Kharg Island destruction. 320K bpd capacity offline. Port area also targeted by US Navy strikes.

facilities.name: Bushehr Port & Nuclear Area
facilities.notes: US-Israeli airstrike on port city confirmed by liveuamap footage (Mar 11-12). Extent of damage unclear. Nuclear plant status not confirmed damaged.

facilities.name: Assaluyeh / South Pars Gas Complex
facilities.notes: No confirmed strikes on this facility. South Pars is the world's largest natural gas field (shared with Qatar's North Field). Strategic target but no damage reports yet.

facilities.name: Abadan Refinery
facilities.notes: No direct damage reports. Located further north near Iraq border. Operations status unclear due to overall conflict disruption.

facilities.name: Isfahan Refinery
facilities.notes: Deep inland. No confirmed damage reports. Isfahan has nuclear facilities which may have been targeted but refinery status unconfirmed.

facilities.name: Jask Oil Terminal
facilities.notes: New terminal outside Hormuz strait (on Gulf of Oman). No damage reports. Could theoretically bypass Hormuz closure for Iranian exports but operations likely minimal.

facilities.name: ADNOC Ruwais Industrial Complex
facilities.notes: Hit by Iranian drone attack (Mar 8). Fire reported at the complex. 900K bpd refining capacity affected. Ruwais is the UAE's largest refining and petrochemical hub.

facilities.name: Fujairah Oil Terminal & Storage
facilities.notes: Receives oil via Habshan-Fujairah pipeline, bypassing Hormuz. Currently operational and handling increased flow. Key bypass route — 1.1M bpd flowing (2025 avg), theoretical max 1.8M bpd.

facilities.name: Jebel Ali Port
facilities.notes: World's largest man-made port. Operations heavily disrupted by Hormuz closure and drone/missile attacks on Dubai area (Mar 12 interceptions). Not directly struck but operating at reduced capacity.

facilities.name: Abu Dhabi Terminals
facilities.notes: Fire at old Abu Dhabi airport from falling debris of intercepted drone (liveuamap Mar 12). Port operations disrupted. UAE air defenses intercepted 6 ballistic missiles, 7 cruise missiles, 39 drones on Mar 12.

facilities.name: Ras Tanura Oil Terminal
facilities.notes: World's largest offshore oil loading facility. No confirmed direct strikes but Hormuz closure blocks tanker access for Gulf-bound routes. Saudi shifting exports to Yanbu (Red Sea) via East-West pipeline. Drone intercepted heading to Shaybah field (Mar 12).

facilities.name: Dammam / King Abdulaziz Port
facilities.notes: Major commercial port on Persian Gulf coast. Operations disrupted by Hormuz closure. No direct damage reports.

facilities.name: Jubail Industrial City
facilities.notes: Major petrochemical and refining complex on Gulf coast. No direct strikes confirmed. Export operations hampered by Hormuz closure.

facilities.name: Ras Laffan Industrial City
facilities.notes: World's largest LNG export facility. Hit by Iranian drone attack (Mar 2-3). QatarEnergy halted ALL production — LNG, urea, polymers, methanol. ~5.8M tonnes LNG removed from March supply (~14% of global monthly). Facility may be structurally intact but production completely halted.

facilities.name: Doha Port
facilities.notes: Commercial port operations severely reduced. Qatar heavily dependent on Hormuz for all maritime trade. Food imports disrupted.

facilities.name: Sohar Port & Industrial Area
facilities.notes: Located outside Hormuz on Gulf of Oman coast. Operational but handling overflow traffic. Key alternative port for regional trade bypassing the strait.

facilities.name: Port Sultan Qaboos (Muscat)
facilities.notes: Outside Hormuz. Operational. MKD Vyom was struck off Muscat (Mar 1) but port itself undamaged. Serving as anchorage for stranded vessels.

facilities.name: Duqm Port & Refinery
facilities.notes: Well south of Hormuz on Arabian Sea coast. Operational. Strategic alternative for regional refining. Oman-Kuwait joint venture refinery.

facilities.name: Sitra Refinery & Terminal
facilities.notes: Stena Imperative struck at Bahrain port (Mar 1). Interception sounds heard over Manama (Mar 12). Refinery operations likely reduced. Bahrain dependent on Saudi oil via pipeline for feedstock.

facilities.name: Shuaiba Port & Refinery
facilities.notes: Sonangol Namibe explosion at Mubarak Al Kabeer Port, Kuwait with oil spill (Mar 2). Iraqi tankers attacked off southern Iraq near Kuwait waters. Kuwait beginning production shut-ins.

facilities.name: Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline
facilities.notes: Critical Hormuz bypass. Carries Abu Dhabi crude from Habshan to Fujairah on Gulf of Oman coast, bypassing the strait. Currently flowing ~1.1M bpd (2025 avg). Theoretical max 1.8M bpd. UAE's lifeline for continued oil exports.

facilities.name: East-West Petroline (Saudi)
facilities.notes: Crosses Saudi Arabia from Eastern Province to Yanbu on Red Sea. Currently flowing ~1.4M bpd (Feb 2026), only ~28% of 5M bpd capacity. Key bypass route but output goes to Red Sea (Houthi risk area). Saudi Arabia could scale up significantly.
